architecture.summary: Adaptive elastic net SAEs (AEN-SAEs) are sparse autoencoders that combine an ℓ2 structural regularization term enforcing strong convexity and Lipschitz stability with adaptive ℓ1 reweighting to suppress spurious features and eliminate shrinkage bias. The encoder maps dense LLM activations to a sparse, monosemantic feature dictionary; the decoder reconstructs activations from selected features. Information flows from frozen LLM activations through a learned encoder (sparse) to a learned decoder, with the ℓ2+adaptive-ℓ1 loss controlling the geometry of the sparse coding map to prevent feature starvation.

biblio.title: Feature Starvation as Geometric Instability in Sparse Autoencoders

claims.statement: Feature starvation is a fundamental optimization-geometric pathology of overcomplete dictionaries: the ℓ1-induced sparse coding map is unstable and fundamentally misaligned with shallow, amortized encoders
AEN-SAEs yield a Lipschitz-continuous sparse coding map and recover the global feature support under mild assumptions
AEN-SAEs mitigate feature starvation without auxiliary heuristics while maintaining competitive reconstruction abilities
Adaptive ℓ1 reweighting eliminates shrinkage bias and suppresses spurious features
The ℓ2 structural term enforces strong convexity and Lipschitz stability, jointly controlling the curvature and interaction structure of the induced polyhedral geometry
AEN-SAEs are evaluated across synthetic settings and LLMs (Pythia 70M, Llama 3.1 8B)

Standard ℓ1-regularized SAEs suffer from feature starvation (dead neurons) and shrinkage bias, often requiring computationally expensive heuristic resampling and nondifferentiable hard-masking methods
